title: US-Iran Agree to Stand Down After Weekend Strikes, Supreme Court Expands Presidential Power While Blocking Trump on Fed and Ballots, Putin Admits Fuel Crisis From Ukrainian Attacks
subhead: Washington and Tehran agree to halt strikes and send negotiators to Doha, but the ceasefire remains fragile. The US Supreme Court overturns 90 years of precedent on presidential removal power — then dealt Trump defeats on the Fed and mail-in ballots. Putin publicly acknowledges Russian fuel shortages for the first time. Pakistan kills at least 28 Afghan civilians in cross-border strikes. Venezuela's earthquake death toll passes 1,700.

sections.heading: US-Iran: Stand-Down Agreed After Weekend Exchange of Strikes
sections.body:
A US official told CBS News on Sunday that both Washington and Tehran had agreed to 'stand down' following several days of reciprocal strikes. The announcement comes after a volatile 72 hours: on Thursday, an Iranian projectile hit a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz, prompting the US to launch retaliatory strikes on multiple Iranian targets. Iran then fired on US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain on Saturday. The US said none of the Iranian attacks reached their targets and there were no casualties or damage, according to the BBC.

Trump announced on Truth Social that Iran had 'requested a meeting' and that talks would take place in Doha. White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt confirmed that special envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner would fly to Qatar for 'high-level meetings this week' to continue discussing the memorandum of understanding. Under the MoU, Iran agreed to use its 'best efforts' for safe passage of commercial vessels for 60 days — a clause that has clearly not prevented continued attacks on shipping.

Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi denied that technical talks were scheduled for this week, creating a familiar pattern: the US announces talks, Iran denies or delays, then both sides eventually show up. The broader question remains whether either side has the ability or intention to enforce a ceasefire that has already collapsed twice since it was signed less than two weeks ago.
sections.whyItMatters: The stand-down is the most positive signal in days, but the gap between 'agreeing to stand down' and actually stopping attacks has been the defining feature of this conflict. The Doha talks represent a continuation of the same negotiating track that produced the Islamabad MoU — which failed within days. The inclusion of Kushner signals Trump's personal investment in the process.

sections.heading: Supreme Court Expands Presidential Power, Then Limits Trump
sections.body:
In a dramatic final week of the term, the Supreme Court overturned nearly 90 years of precedent on Monday, ruling 6-3 that presidents have broad authority to remove regulators from independent agencies. The decision scraps the 1935 Humphrey's Executor precedent and gives Trump — and all future presidents — sweeping power to fire officials at the FTC, NLRB, FCC, and dozens of other agencies. Chief Justice Roberts wrote that 'subordinates who exercise the president's power are subject to removal by him.' Trump celebrated on Truth Social, calling it a decision that 'greatly increases presidential power at a time when it is most needed.'

But the court then drew sharp limits. In a 5-4 ruling, Roberts and Kavanaugh joined the three liberal justices to block Trump's attempt to remove Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook, with Roberts warning of the 'calamities that could arise' if presidents could impose their will on the central bank. And in a separate case, Amy Coney Barrett wrote for a 5-4 majority (with Roberts again crossing the aisle) to uphold states' ability to count mail-in ballots received after election day — a direct defeat for Trump's election-reform agenda ahead of the midterms.

The court also declined to hear Trump's appeal of the $5m E. Jean Carroll defamation judgment, effectively ending that legal battle. The net result: a major expansion of presidential removal power over regulators, but a clear line drawn at the Fed and at state election laws — with conservative justices providing the swing votes against Trump.
sections.whyItMatters: The FTC ruling fundamentally reshapes the administrative state, giving presidents far more control over regulatory agencies. But the court's willingness to constrain Trump on the Fed and elections — with conservative justices in the majority — signals that the expanded presidential power has boundaries. The mail-in ballot ruling could directly affect the midterms in over a dozen states.

sections.heading: Putin Publicly Acknowledges Russian Fuel Shortages From Ukrainian Strikes
sections.body:
In a rare public admission, Vladimir Putin acknowledged that Ukraine's long-range strikes on Russian energy infrastructure are causing domestic fuel shortages. Speaking to a meeting of officials and oil executives over the weekend, Putin said 'problems persist for both motorists and businesses,' noting that 'there are still queues at petrol stations, and finding the right grade of petrol isn't always easy.' In a subsequent state television interview, he conceded that Ukraine's attacks were 'obviously creating problems' and that Russia was 'seeing a certain shortage' — though he insisted it was 'not critical.'

According to independent Russian outlet Mediazona, 56 Russian regions are currently enforcing fuel restrictions. Putin admitted that Crimea had only 'a few days' supply' of fuel left — a striking disclosure given the peninsula's symbolic and military importance to the Kremlin since its 2014 annexation. He promised to increase air defence production and speed up refinery repairs.

Putin framed his transparency as pre-emptive: he accused Ukraine of trying to 'divide Russian society' and 'increase support for negotiations,' saying 'we won't give them that chance.' The Kremlin confirmed its war aims remain unchanged — forcing Ukraine to leave the four southeastern regions Moscow claims as its own.
sections.whyItMatters: Putin's acknowledgment of fuel shortages — and the specific mention of Crimea's precarious supply — marks a shift in Kremlin messaging. The admission suggests the domestic impact of Ukrainian strikes has become too visible to deny. Whether this transparency reflects confidence or strain is the key question: if 56 regions are rationing fuel, the 'not critical' framing may not hold for long.

sections.heading: Pakistan Kills at Least 28 Afghan Civilians in Cross-Border Strikes
sections.body:
Pakistan launched air strikes and sent ground troops into Afghanistan's Paktia, Paktika, and Kunar provinces on Sunday, killing at least 28 civilians according to the UN Afghan mission (Unama). A further 49 were injured, with women and children among the victims. The Taliban government put the death toll at 36 with over 160 injured, and described the attack as a 'cowardly act' and an 'atrocity.' Casualties were concentrated in the village of Mandokhail in Paktia province.

Pakistan said it targeted militant hideouts, with Information Minister Attaullah Tarar claiming 29 militants were killed. The operation came one day after three members of the Sindh Rangers paramilitary force were killed in a suicide attack on their Karachi headquarters, claimed by Jamaat-ul-Ahrar, a TTP splinter faction. One of the captured attackers was identified as an Afghan national.

This is the second major cross-border operation in June alone. A previous strike earlier in the month killed 26 militants, but Afghanistan said 13 civilians — mostly children — also died. An October 2025 ceasefire between the two countries has effectively collapsed, with intermittent clashes killing dozens in recent months.
sections.whyItMatters: The Pakistan-Afghanistan border conflict continues to intensify despite multiple ceasefire attempts. Each Pakistani strike produces significant civilian casualties, generating domestic pressure on the Taliban to retaliate — but also giving Islamabad justification for further action. The cycle of attack and counter-attack is becoming entrenched, with no functional diplomatic mechanism to break it.

sections.heading: Venezuela Earthquake Toll Passes 1,700 as Rescuers Race Against Time
sections.body:
The death toll from Venezuela's twin earthquakes has reached at least 1,700, according to interim President Delcy Rodríguez, who called it the 'most brutal natural catastrophe' in the country's history. The magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 quakes struck 39 seconds apart on Wednesday in La Guaira state, collapsing nearly 800 buildings. Tens of thousands remain missing. A magnitude 4.6 aftershock on Monday frayed nerves but caused no further damage, the BBC reported.

Rescue efforts remain largely volunteer-driven, with residents using crowbars, mallets, and bare hands. The BBC observed that police and military were present on streets in affected areas but not in the rubble. Heavy equipment has been arriving late or not at all. Carolyn Zerpa, searching for her father and brother, told BBC Mundo: 'You can't really do much with just a pickaxe.'

International aid is mobilising: the US has pledged over $300m, up from $150m, with the USS Fort Lauderdale positioned off La Guaira delivering supplies via landing craft. China has promised almost $15m. The UN is procuring 10,000 body bags, according to humanitarian coordinator Gianluca Rampolla Del Tindaro, who said the death toll was 'unavoidably' going to rise. At least 2,500 structures have been affected, most fully collapsed.
sections.whyItMatters: The final death toll could be catastrophic — potentially among the worst earthquake disasters in Western Hemisphere history. The gap between international aid pledges and on-the-ground rescue capability remains vast, with the Venezuelan government's response drawing sharp criticism. The political consequences for Rodríguez are building as public frustration mounts.

sections.heading: Europe's Heatwave: 1,300 Dead and Counting, Now Shifting East
sections.body:
The World Health Organization has linked approximately 1,300 excess deaths to the heatwave that began on June 20 and has gripped Europe for over a week. France alone has reported over 1,000 excess deaths, with its public health agency warning the number will rise. Funeral homes in Paris and the surrounding region have struggled to cope with the volume of bodies, French media reported. The WHO noted that Europe is the fastest-warming continent on Earth, heating at twice the global average.

Italy placed 22 cities under red heat warnings on Monday, from Bolzano in the north to Palermo in Sicily. Croatia issued red alerts for Zagreb, Split, and Dubrovnik. On the Adriatic island of Vis, dozens of firefighters battled a wildfire through pine forests. Much of the Balkans remained under extreme heat, with temperatures above 35°C across Croatia, Serbia, Romania, and Hungary. Italian Air Force meteorologist Daniele Mocio said temperatures would remain 8-10°C above average for several more days, with another surge expected around July 5-6.

Scientists said the heatwave would have been 'virtually impossible' without human-caused climate change, which has made this week's extreme night-time temperatures 100 times more likely than they would have been two decades ago.
sections.whyItMatters: Over 1,300 dead in nine days makes this one of the deadliest heat events in European history — and it is not over. A second surge predicted for early July means the toll will continue to climb. The event is exposing the limits of Europe's heat adaptation infrastructure and will intensify pressure on climate policy across the continent.

bottomLine: The US and Iran agreed to stand down after a weekend of reciprocal strikes, with Doha talks set for this week — but the ceasefire has already failed twice since signing. The US Supreme Court handed Trump a landmark expansion of presidential removal power over independent agencies, then dealt him three separate defeats on the Fed, mail-in ballots, and the E. Jean Carroll case — with conservative justices providing the swing votes against him. Putin publicly admitted Russian fuel shortages from Ukrainian strikes for the first time, a notable shift in Kremlin messaging. Pakistan killed at least 28 Afghan civilians in cross-border strikes. Venezuela's earthquake toll passed 1,700 with tens of thousands still missing. Europe's heatwave death toll reached 1,300 and is climbing. Multiple crises are running simultaneously with no sign of abating.
